Emotion Regulation Worksheet 5   (Emotion Regulation Handouts 8, 8a) (p. 1 of 2)
Check the Facts
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
It is hard to problem-solve an emotional situation if you donât have your facts straight. You must
know what the problem is before you can solve it. This worksheet helps you figure out whether
it is the event that is causing your emotion, your interpretation of the event, or both. Use your
mindfulness skills of observing and describing. Observe the facts, and then describe the facts you
have observed.
Step
1

Ask: What emotion do I want to change?

2

Ask: What is the prompting event for my emotional reaction?
Describe the prompting event: What happened that led you to have this
emotion? Who did what to whom? What led up to what? What is it about this event that
is a problem for you? Be very specific in your answers.


Check the Facts!
Look for extremes and judgments in the way you are describing the prompting event.
Rewrite the facts, if necessary, to be more accurate.
Facts

Ask: What are my interpretations (thoughts, beliefs, etc.) about the facts?
What am I assuming? Am I adding my own interpretations to the description of the
prompting event?


Check the Facts!
List as many other possible interpretations of the facts as you can.


Rewrite the facts, if necessary. Try to check the accuracy of your interpretations. If you
canât check the facts, write out a likely or a useful (i.e., effective) interpretation.
Facts

Ask: Am I assuming a threat? What is the threat? What about this event or
situation is threatening to me? What worrisome consequences or outcomes am I
expecting?


Check the Facts!
List as many other possible outcomes as you can, given the facts.


Rewrite the facts if needed. Try to check the accuracy of your expectations. If you
canât check out probable outcomes, write out a likely noncatastrophic outcome to
expect.


Facts

Ask: Whatâs the catastrophe, even if the outcome I am worrying about does
occur? Describe in detail the worst outcome I can reasonably expect.


Describe ways to cope if the worst does happen.


Step
6

Ask: Does my emotion (or its intensity or duration) fit the facts?
(0 = not at all to 5 = I am certain):
If you are unsure whether your emotion or your emotional intensity fits the facts (for
example, you give a score of 2, 3, or 4), keep checking the facts. Be as creative as you
can be; ask others for their opinions; or do an experiment to see if your predictions or
interpretations are correct.
Describe what you did to check the facts:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: It is hard to problem-solve an emotional situation if you donât have your facts straight. You must know what the problem is before you can solve it. This worksheet helps you figure out whether it is the event that is causing your emotion, your interpretation of the event, or both. Use your mindfulness skills of observing and describing. Observe the facts, and then describe the facts you
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.
